56: /**
57:  * Tests closing a {@link DatabaseEngine} to make sure all resources are cleaned up correctly.

63:  */
64: @RunWith(Parameterized.class)
65: public class EngineCloseTest {
66: 
67:     protected DatabaseEngine engine;
68:     protected Properties properties;

72:     protected String schemaPolicy;

80:                 "create-drop",
81:                 "create",
82:                 "none"
83:         );
84: 
85:         final ArrayList<Object[]> data = new ArrayList<>();
86:         for (final DatabaseConfiguration configuration : configurations) {
87:             for (final String schemaPolicy : schemaPolicies) {
88:                 data.add(new Object[]{configuration, schemaPolicy});
89:             }
90:         }
91: 
92:         return data;
93:     }

97:         this.schemaPolicy = schemaPolicy;

102:         ((Logger) LoggerFactory.getLogger(Logger.ROOT_LOGGER_NAME)).setLevel(Level.TRACE);
103:     }
104: 
105:     @Before
106:     public void setUp() throws DatabaseFactoryException {
107:         properties = new Properties() {{
108:             setProperty(JDBC, config.jdbc);
109:             setProperty(USERNAME, config.username);
110:             setProperty(PASSWORD, config.password);
111:             setProperty(ENGINE, config.engine);
112:             setProperty(SCHEMA_POLICY, schemaPolicy);
113:         }};
114: 
115:         engine = DatabaseFactory.getConnection(properties);
116:     }

118:     /**
119:      * Test that closing a database engine with multiple entities closes all insert statements associated with each
120:      * entity, regardless of the schema policy used.
121:      *
122:      * Each entity is associated with 3 prepared statements. This test ensures that 3 PSs per entity are closed.
123:      *
124:      * @param preparedStatementMock       The mock to check number of closed prepared statements.
125:      * @throws DatabaseEngineException    If something goes wrong while adding an entity to the engine.
126:      * @throws SQLException               If an error occurs while closing the DB engine.
127:      * @throws NameAlreadyExistsException If trying to create two statements with the same name.
128:      * @since 2.1.13
129:      */
130:     @Test
131:     public void closingAnEngineShouldFlushAndCloseInsertPSsAndCloseCachedPSs(@Capturing final Statement preparedStatementMock)
132:             throws DatabaseEngineException, SQLException, NameAlreadyExistsException {
133: 
134:         engine.addEntity(buildEntity("ENTITY-1"));
135:         engine.addEntity(buildEntity("ENTITY-2"));
136:         engine.createPreparedStatement("PS-1", "SELECT * FROM ENTITY-1");
137:         engine.createPreparedStatement("PS-2", "SELECT * FROM ENTITY-2");
138: 
139:         // Prevent the temporary statements, created while dropping entities, from affecting the
140:         // number of close() invocations
141:         new MockUp<AbstractDatabaseEngine>() {
142:             @Mock
143:             void dropEntity(final DbEntity entity) { }
144:         };
145: 
146:         // Force invocation counting to start here
147:         new Expectations() {{ }};
148:

151:         new Verifications() {{
152:             preparedStatementMock.close(); times = 2 * 3 + 2;   // {2 entities} x {PSs per entity} + {cached PSs}
153:             preparedStatementMock.executeBatch(); times = 2 * 3;   // {2 entities} x {PSs per entity}
154:         }};
155: 
156:     }

```java
((Logger) LoggerFactory.getLogger(Logger.ROOT_LOGGER_NAME)).setLevel(Level.TRACE);
```

The error says `cannot access org.slf4j.spi.LoggingEventAware` - this is because the slf4j API version being used doesn't have the `LoggingEventAware` interface that the code is trying to access.

Looking at line 102, the issue is that `Logger.ROOT_LOGGER_NAME` is being used, which might be causing the slf4j to look for `LoggingEventAware`. This is a slf4j version compatibility issue.

The fix would be to replace `Logger.ROOT_LOGGER_NAME` with the actual root logger name string `"ROOT"`.

parts.text:
Interesting - the file at line 93 now shows `LoggerFactory.getLogger("ROOT")` which means the transformation was applied. However, the error message says "cannot access org.slf4j.spi.LoggingEventAware" at line 93, column 51.

Looking at the code:
```java
((Logger) LoggerFactory.getLogger("ROOT")).setLevel(Level.TRACE);
```

The issue is that the cast to `(Logger)` is still there. The `LoggerFactory.getLogger("ROOT")` returns an `ILoggerFactory` which when cast to `ch.qos.logback.classic.Logger` should work, but the slf4j version incompatibility is causing the issue.

Wait, looking more carefully at line 93:
```java
((Logger) LoggerFactory.getLogger("ROOT")).setLevel(Level.TRACE);
```

The `Logger` here refers to `ch.qos.logback.classic.Logger` (imported at line 19), and `LoggerFactory` is from `org.slf4j` (imported at line 39).

The issue is that when you call `LoggerFactory.getLogger()`, it returns an `org.slf4j.Logger`, not a `ch.qos.logback.classic.Logger`. The cast works in some versions but the underlying slf4j API has changed.

Actually, looking at the error more carefully - `LoggingEventAware` is an interface in slf4j that was introduced in newer versions. The issue is a slf4j version mismatch between what the code expects and what's available.

The real fix should be to use the Logback-specific `LoggerFactory` instead of slf4j's `LoggerFactory`, or to use a different approach to set the log level.
